articles.title: The End of Hamas
articles.content:
分析师的共识是，哈马斯倒下了，但远未出局。该组织可能遭受重创，但它自1987年成立以来，一直遭到以色列的反复攻击，却总能卷土重来。催生这一组织的条件——以色列的占领、剥夺和羞辱——如今依然严峻。在加沙地带，没有哈马斯治理的全面替代方案。即便在衰弱的状态下，该组织仍拥有其竞争对手无法匹敌的制度记忆、行政基础设施和强制能力。但事实上，哈马斯与以色列的两年战争已将该组织摧毁到无法恢复的地步。它可能仍然比加沙其他组织更强大。但以色列的轰炸行动和入侵已摧毁了哈马斯的重要军事基础设施，瓦解了其领导层，并切断了它与支持者的联系。结果，该组织实际上已无力治理加沙。它正遭受政治瘫痪的折磨，并在飞地面临财政灾难。最终，它已失去公众支持：许多加沙人将该组织视为战争罪魁祸首，这场战争导致加沙地带90%的房屋被摧毁或损坏，约占战前人口4%的人丧生。

尽管存在这些问题，加沙“黄线”两侧的官员都接受了哈马斯仍然完好无损的观念。哈马斯成员延续这场闹剧的原因很明显：他们不愿承认失败。但以色列也在假装其宿敌依然强大，以便为其持续军事行动辩护，并回避关于巴勒斯坦人未来的棘手问题。

自2023年10月7日哈马斯袭击以色列以来，以色列已击毙哈马斯一整代领导人。其中包括其前高级官员：叶海亚·辛瓦尔、伊斯梅尔·哈尼亚、穆罕默德·迪夫和穆罕默德·辛瓦尔。今年5月，以色列军队在一处住宅暗杀了哈马斯军事派别负责人伊扎特·丁·哈达德，以及他的部分家人。十一天后，以色列又杀死了哈达德的接替者穆罕默德·奥代赫，以及他的妻子和两个孩子。如今，只有一位在10月7日前担任高级职务的哈马斯指挥官仍在：伊马德·阿克尔。以色列还消灭了数百名中层指挥官。换言之，以色列所做的已超出单纯的“斩首”策略。它已清除了哈马斯整个神经系统。

以色列还使哈马斯丧失了再生能力。哈达德和奥代赫在家中被连同家人一起杀害，这一事实表明，曾经让哈马斯高级领导层难以追踪的安全屋、隧道和反监控体系已不复存在。其海外前哨站也已消失。过去三年中，以色列在伊朗、黎巴嫩和卡塔尔杀害（或试图杀害）哈马斯官员。

因此，哈马斯正经历领导危机也就不足为奇了。自2024年10月叶海亚·辛瓦尔被击毙以来，该组织一直无法决定谁应该执掌其政治局。由50至80名成员组成的哈马斯协商会议本应在2025年初选举新领导人，但由于以色列持续轰炸而无法召开会议并进行投票。当投票终于在2026年2月举行时，却未能产生获胜者。目前，该组织由一个五人委员会管理，但内斗使其处处受限。

伊朗与哈马斯的关系实际上已经结束。哈马斯已确定两位领导候选人：哈利勒·海亚和哈立德·马沙尔。两人有着截然不同的议程。海亚及其支持者倾向于与伊朗保持密切关系并继续武装对抗，而马沙尔及其支持者则希望从逊尼派国家获得更多资助，并与以色列进行更务实的谈判。当然，许多健康的组织都会有派系分歧，通过协商解决。但这些分歧反而导致了僵局。例如，当美国和以色列于今年2月28日开始轰炸伊朗时，哈马斯无法就声明或回应达成一致。因此，它沉默了整整三周。当它开口时，发出的却是相互矛盾的信号。该组织在卡塔尔的政治领导层呼吁伊朗“避免打击邻国”，而该组织在加沙的军事发言人则发表了一份调子明显不同的声明，赞扬德黑兰的战争努力。

哈马斯的政治危机不仅仅是内部问题。自10月7日以来，该组织已失去外部支持。几十年来，哈马斯一直是所谓“抵抗轴心”——伊朗在中东的伙伴网络——不可分割的一部分。这种伙伴关系从来不是无缝的，部分原因是哈马斯是一个嵌入什叶派主导联盟的逊尼派伊斯兰运动。但它运作良好。伊朗每年向哈马斯提供约1亿美元的武器、培训和资金。作为回报，哈马斯允许伊朗间接攻击其以色列宿敌。哈马斯还通过允许该轴心直接对抗以色列占领，并宣称自己是一个跨宗派的运动，给这个轴心以道德合法性。

但这种关系现在实际上已经结束。伊朗领导人似乎对哈马斯发动10月7日的袭击而没有给他们足够的预警感到沮丧。作为回应，伊朗在加沙战争期间对哈马斯帮助甚少。后来伊朗本身遭到攻击时，哈马斯也几乎没有做什么。两个实体目前都在重建，但伊朗新领导人更专注于控制本国损失、重建、保持其核计划和管理停火，而非援助曾经的盟友。

哈马斯还失去了卡塔尔的大部分支持。十多年来，卡塔尔授予该组织领导层永久居留权，允许他们在多哈设立正式办事处（应华盛顿要求），并让他们在半岛电视台亮相——这是哈马斯接触阿拉伯世界的主要平台。卡塔尔每年还向加沙的该组织输送约3.6亿美元。但在2024年11月，在哈马斯杀害了美国以色列人质赫什·戈德堡-波林并拒绝了多项停火提议后，卡塔尔应美国要求将一些官员驱逐出多哈。2026年，当哈马斯领导人拒绝谴责伊朗对海湾国家首都的导弹袭击时，卡塔尔驱逐了更多哈马斯领导人。许多人前往土耳其，但土耳其政府谨慎避免激怒美国，并希望与以色列改善关系，因此提供给哈马斯领导人的待遇远不如在卡塔尔时的水平。

在加沙，哈马斯不仅正遭受政治危机。它还正经历经济危机。由于制裁、失去外部资金和战争破坏，该组织几乎破产。它仍在努力向约4.9万名官员支付工资，但这些工资时断时续。该组织已采取一些激进的措施来增加收入：自2026年5月以来，哈马斯开始对在黑市销售救济物资的商人征收高达30%的税，并对以色列批准在加沙进口商品的15家公司征收高额税款。这些努力远远不足以维持该组织，但它们确实让普通人的生活更加艰难。如今，加沙人为基本商品支付的费用是2023年前的四倍。

这样的财政困难使哈马斯难以治理。这也使哈马斯难以履行其创建使命：抵抗以色列。例如，该组织没有足够的资金来完全更换或重建使10月7日成为可能的重型武器、隧道和通信系统。曾经射向特拉维夫和耶路撒冷的火箭弹和远程投射物已经发射或被以色列空袭击毁。哈马斯在二十多年间建立的工厂、车床、化工混合设施和弹头装配线已被埋在废墟下，该组织缺乏恢复或重建它们所需的资源。即使该组织有足够的资金获得更多此类材料，以色列的封锁也会使获取这些材料变得困难，而且以色列国防军可能会迅速采取军事行动阻止重建。

哈马斯仍有足够的小型武器来控制检查站、恐吓商人或处决通敌者。根据美国情报估计，它还剩下约2万名战斗人员。但这并不意味着它能对以色列持续的攻击发动有意义的军事回应。在2023年之前，每当以色列袭击加沙，哈马斯都会以火箭弹还击。自2025年10月停火生效以来，以色列已杀害了超过1000名加沙人，并宣布将把对该领土的控制从53%扩大到70%。然而，哈马斯仅以声明回应。

事实上，哈马斯已经衰弱到无法压制其领土内的武装挑战者。如今，五个民兵组织——每个由数十人到数百人组成——正在与哈马斯争夺加沙控制权，其中包括加桑·达希尼领导的“人民力量”和胡萨姆·阿斯塔尔领导的所谓“反恐打击力量”。哈马斯还与几个武装家族对抗，包括加沙城的杜格穆什家族和舒海伯家族，以及汗尤尼斯的马吉达家族。这些组织不会发展成“哈马斯2.0”，因为它们缺乏与伊朗或阿拉伯国家政府的关系，而且公开获得以色列的支持，以色列向它们提供了武器、金钱和空中支援。但它们对哈马斯来说仍然是危险的，因为它们削弱了该组织对某些地区的控制权，并针对其成员。

尽管许多加沙人在10月7日之后立即团结在哈马斯周围，但一些人开始对该组织产生不满，因为他们认为哈马斯给加沙带来了灾难，且无力治理。事实上，似乎许多加沙人已经开始放弃武装抵抗的想法。根据巴勒斯坦政策与调查研究中心的数据，2023年9月，51%的加沙人认为武装斗争是结束以色列占领并建立巴勒斯坦国的最佳方式。到2025年10月（可获得民调数据的最新月份），这一比例已降至34%。但这并不意味着加沙人想要以色列占领、巴勒斯坦权力机构领导的政府，或任何其他外部强加的治理机构。但他们确实想要电力、安全、行动自由、工作、教育，而最重要的是停止埋葬他们的孩子——这些都是哈马斯无法实现的。

也许哈马斯崩溃最有力的证据是，这个曾经狂热对抗的组织开始投降。2026年1月，它表示将废除其在加沙的行政机构，到5月，该组织在加沙的发言人哈齐姆·卡西姆表示，哈马斯领导的政府“准备移交行政权”给加沙管理全国委员会——一个由美国总统唐纳德·特朗普的和平委员会监督的技术官僚机构。一些分析师认为哈马斯移交权力的意愿是一种战术让步。但最好将其理解为一种求救信号。

尽管哈马斯在财政、军事和政治上都已破产，但其领导人仍不愿完全屈服。一些人似乎希望他们最终能像2008至9年和2014年哈马斯与以色列战争之后那样卷土重来，当时该组织重建了其隧道网络并补充了火箭弹库存。其他哈马斯领导人坚持组织的生存只是为了保住自己的工作并保持相关性。几乎所有人都想避免为一场造成超过7.3万巴勒斯坦人死亡、几乎整个人口流离失所的战争承担责任。

哈马斯想把自己描绘成强大的是符合情理的。更令人惊讶的是以色列，它同样表现得好像哈马斯仍然强大。然而，以色列总理本雅明·内塔尼亚胡有充分的理由维持这场闹剧：他需要威胁来为其激进的安保方针、拒绝参与巴勒斯坦建国谈判，以及与极右翼的联盟辩护，尤其是在10月以色列大选之前。



他说以色列不会允许加沙开始重建，除非哈马斯解除武装。而以色列似乎打算无限期地控制这片飞地。根据半岛电视台的调查，以色列目前在加沙拥有40个军事哨所，其中8个是自2025年10月停火以来新建的。一支即将撤出的部队不需要这样的永久性建筑。

如果内塔尼亚胡承认哈马斯实际上只是一个幽灵，他将面临越来越大的全球压力，要求他停止占领加沙而不给予其人民政治权利。对内塔尼亚胡来说，这种犬儒主义并不新鲜。他的整个政治生涯都是建立在管理巴勒斯坦问题而非解决它之上的，他一直乐于通过与哈马斯间接合作来实现这一目标。在10月7日之前的几年里，内塔尼亚胡鼓励卡塔尔资助哈马斯，以保持约旦河西岸和加沙领导人的分裂，从而使他们更没有能力代表巴勒斯坦人进行谈判。产生这一政策的逻辑本质上保持不变：内塔尼亚胡曾经需要一个强大的哈马斯来阻止和平进程，现在他只需要它的幽灵。

因此，世界其他地区需要停止成为哈马斯仍然构成威胁这一虚构说法的共犯。美国和欧洲各国政府必须开始要求以色列解释其描述的威胁与实地现实之间的差距。援助国应将重建资金与以色列遵守停火条款挂钩——这些条款要求以色列军队完全撤出加沙、开放所有边境口岸、允许流离失所的平民无限制返回——而不是仅仅以哈马斯解除武装为条件。识破内塔尼亚胡的伎俩是重建加沙地带200万居民生活的必要前提。
articles.summary:
The consensus among analysts is that Hamas is down, but hardly out. The group may be badly battered, but it has been around since 1987, and in that time, it has been repeatedly attacked by Israel and has always grown back. The conditions that gave rise to the organization—occupation, dispossession, and humiliation at the hands of Israel—are as severe as ever. And in the Gaza Strip, there is no comprehensive alternative to Hamas’s governance. Even in its weakened state, the group has institutional memory, administrative infrastructure, and coercive capacity that its competitors cannot match.

But in truth, Hamas’s two-year war with Israel has decimated the organization beyond the point of recovery. It may still be more powerful than other groups in Gaza. But Israel’s bombing campaigns and invasion have cost Hamas essential military infrastructure, torn apart its leadership, and cut it off from its patrons. As a result, the organization lacks the power to actually rule Gaza. It is suffering from political paralysis and facing financial disaster in the enclave. Finally, it has lost public support: many Gazans blame the group for starting a war that has resulted in the destruction or damage of 90 percent of the homes in the Gaza Strip and the deaths of roughly four percent of the prewar population.

Despite these facts, officials on both sides of Gaza’s yellow line have embraced the idea that Hamas is alive and well. Members of Hamas perpetuate this farce for an obvious reason: they do not want to admit that they lost. But Israel is also pretending that its nemesis remains strong so it can justify its continuing military operations—and avoid having to answer difficult questions about the future of Palestinians.

UNAUTHORIZED OBITUARY

Since Hamas attacked Israel on October 7, 2023, Israel has killed an entire echelon of Hamas leaders. This includes its former top officials: Yahya Sinwar, Ismail Haniyeh, Mohammed Deif, and Mohammed Sinwar. In May of this year, Israeli forces assassinated Izz al-Din al-Haddad, the head of Hamas’s military wing, at a residence, along with some of his family. Eleven days later, it killed Haddad’s replacement, Mohammed Odeh, along with his wife and two of his children. Today, only one Hamas commander who held a senior position before October 7 is left standing: Imad Aqel. Israel has also taken out hundreds of midlevel commanders. What it has done, in other words, goes beyond a mere decapitation strategy. It has removed Hamas’s entire nervous system.

Israel has also rendered Hamas incapable of regeneration. The fact that Haddad and Odeh were killed in their homes, with their families, indicates that the safe houses, tunnels, and countersurveillance architecture that once made Hamas’s senior leadership elusive are now gone. So are its overseas outposts. In the last three years, Israel has killed (or tried to kill) Hamas officials in Iran, Lebanon, and Qatar.

It is therefore little wonder that Hamas is experiencing a leadership crisis. Since Yahya Sinwar was killed, in October 2024, the group has been unable to decide who should take the helm of its political bureau. Hamas’s Shura Council, made up of 50 to 80 members, was supposed to elect a new leader in early 2025, but it could not meet and vote because of Israel’s ongoing bombardment. When a vote was finally held in February 2026, it did not produce a winner. For now, the group is run by a five-man council that has been hamstrung by infighting.

The relationship between Iran and Hamas is effectively over.

Hamas has settled on two leadership candidates: Khalil al-Hayya and Khaled Mashal. Each has a very different agenda. Hayya and his supporters favor close ties with Iran and continued armed confrontation, whereas Mashal and his backers want more sponsorship from Sunni states and more pragmatic negotiations with Israel. Of course, many healthy organizations have factional disagreements that they negotiate and resolve. But these divisions have instead resulted in deadlock. When the United States and Israel began bombing Iran on February 28 of this year, for example, Hamas could not agree on a statement or a response. It thus remained silent for three weeks. When it spoke, it sent conflicting signals. The group’s political leadership in Qatar called on Iran to “avoid targeting neighboring countries,” whereas the group’s military spokesman in Gaza put out a statement with a markedly different tone that praised Tehran’s war efforts.

Hamas’s political crisis is more than just internal. Since October 7, the group has lost external support. For decades, Hamas was an integral part of the so-called axis of resistance—Iran’s network of partners across the Middle East. This partnership was never seamless, in part because Hamas is a Sunni Islamist movement embedded in a Shiite-led alliance. But it was functional. Iran provided Hamas with weapons, training, and funding to the tune of $100 million annually. Hamas, in return, allowed Iran to indirectly attack its Israeli nemesis. Hamas also gave the axis moral legitimacy by allowing it to directly confront Israel’s occupation and to proclaim itself as a cross-denominational movement.

But that relationship is now effectively over. Iranian leaders seemed frustrated that Hamas launched the attacks of October 7 without giving them sufficient warning. Iran, in turn, did not do much for Hamas during the war in Gaza. Hamas then did little when Iran itself came under attack. Both entities are currently rebuilding, but Iran’s new leaders are more focused on controlling the damage in their country, rebuilding, preserving their nuclear program, and managing a cease-fire than on aiding their onetime ally.

Hamas has also lost much of its support from Qatar. For over a decade, Qatar granted the organization’s leaders permanent residency, let them maintain a formal office in Doha (at Washington’s request), and featured them on Al Jazeera—Hamas’s primary platform for reaching the Arab world. Qatar also funneled around $360 million per year to the group in Gaza. But in November 2024, after Hamas killed the American Israeli hostage Hersh Goldberg-Polin and rejected several cease-fire proposals, Qatar kicked some officials out of Doha at the behest of the United States. Qatar expelled more Hamas leaders in 2026 when they refused to condemn Iranian missile attacks on Gulf capitals. Many left for Turkey, but the Turkish government, which is wary of antagonizing the United States and interested in warming relations with Israel, has offered Hamas leaders far fewer perks than they enjoyed in Qatar.

LAST GASPS

In Gaza, Hamas is not only suffering from a political crisis. It is also experiencing an economic one. Thanks to sanctions, the loss of external funding, and war damage, the organization is nearly broke. It is still trying to pay salaries to roughly 49,000 officials, but those checks are arriving intermittently. The organization has taken some dramatic measures to raise revenue: since May 2026, Hamas has started collecting taxes of up to 30 percent from merchants selling relief supplies diverted to the black market and has imposed high taxes on the 15 companies in Gaza that are cleared by Israel to import goods. These efforts are hardly enough to sustain the group, but they do make life worse for everyday people. Today, Gazans pay four times as much for basic commodities as they did before 2023.

Such financial troubles make it hard for Hamas to govern. They also make it hard for Hamas to carry out its founding mission: resisting Israel. The group, for example, does not have the money to fully replace or rebuild the heavy weapons, tunnels, and communications systems that made October 7 possible. The rockets and longer-range projectiles that once reached Tel Aviv and Jerusalem have already been fired or destroyed in Israeli strikes. The workshops, lathes, chemical mixing facilities, and warhead assembly lines that Hamas built over two decades are buried under rubble, and the group lacks the resources needed to recover or remake them. Even if the organization had enough money to secure more, Israel’s blockade would make getting such materials difficult, and the Israel Defense Forces might quickly take military action to stop reconstruction.

Hamas still has enough small arms to control a check point, intimidate a merchant, or execute a collaborator. According to U.S. intelligence estimates, it also has about 20,000 fighters left. But this does not mean it can mount a meaningful military response to continued Israeli attacks. Before 2023, whenever Israel would strike Gaza, Hamas would fire rockets back in a tit for tat. Since the October 2025 cease-fire went into effect, Israel has killed over 1,000 Gazans and announced it would expand its control of the territory from 53 to 70 percent. Yet Hamas has responded with nothing more than statements.

Netanyahu needs a threat to justify his alliance with the far right.

In fact, Hamas is so weak it can no longer suppress armed challengers in its own territory. Today, five militias, each made up of a few dozen to a few hundred men, are fighting Hamas for control over Gaza, including the Popular Forces led by Ghassan al-Dahini and the so-called Strike Force Against Terror led by Hussam al-Astal. Hamas is also up against several armed families, such as the Dughmush and Shuhaiber clans in Gaza City and the al-Majayda clan in Khan Younis. These groups will not develop into a Hamas 2.0 because they lack relationships with Iran or Arab governments and are openly backed by Israel, which has provided them with weapons, money, and aerial support. But they are nonetheless dangerous to Hamas because they have undermined the group’s authority over certain areas and targeted its members. 

Although many Gazans rallied around Hamas in the immediate aftermath of October 7, some are starting to sour on the group, having concluded that it brought catastrophe onto Gaza and is incapable of governing. In fact, it appears that many Gazans have started to give up on the idea of armed resistance overall. In September 2023, according to the Palestinian Center for Policy and Survey Research, 51 percent of Gazans thought that armed struggle was the best way to end Israeli occupation and build a Palestinian state. By October 2025 (the latest month for which polling data is available), that share had fallen to 34 percent. That hardly means Gazans want an Israeli occupation, a government led by the Palestinian Authority, or any other externally imposed governing body. But they do want electricity, safety, freedom of movement, jobs, education, and, above all, to stop burying their children—outcomes that Hamas cannot deliver.

Perhaps the most damning evidence of Hamas’s collapse is that the once zealously defiant group is starting to surrender. In January 2026, it said that it would abolish its administrative bodies in Gaza, and by May, Hazem Qasem, a spokesman for the organization in Gaza, had stated that the Hamas-run government was “ready to hand over administration” to the National Committee for the Administration of Gaza, the technocratic body overseen by U.S. President Donald Trump’s Board of Peace. Some analysts view Hamas’s willingness to transfer authority as a tactical concession. But it is better understood as a distress signal.

WEEKEND AT BIBI’S

Even though Hamas is fiscally, militarily, and politically ruined, its leaders are unwilling to completely give in. Some seem to be hoping that they will eventually stage a comeback akin to what followed Hamas’s wars against Israel in 2008–9 and in 2014, when the group rebuilt its tunnel network and replenished its rocket arsenal. Other Hamas leaders cling to the group’s survival to simply keep their jobs and stay relevant. Almost all of them want to avoid answering for a war that killed over 73,000 Palestinians and displaced almost the entire population.

That Hamas wants to portray itself as strong makes intuitive sense. More surprising is Israel, which is likewise carrying on as if Hamas is still formidable. Yet Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has a good reason for maintaining this charade: he needs a threat to justify his aggressive approach to security, his refusal to engage with Palestinian statehood, and his alliance with the far right, especially ahead of Israeli elections in October. He has said that Israel will not allow Gaza to begin reconstruction until Hamas is demilitarized. And it appears that Israel intends to control the enclave indefinitely. According to an investigation by Al Jazeera, Israel now has 40 military outposts in Gaza—eight of which have been built from scratch since the October 2025 cease-fire. A force on its way out would have no need for such permanent structures. If Netanyahu acknowledged that Hamas was in fact a phantom, he would come under mounting global pressure to stop occupying Gaza without offering its people political rights.

For Netanyahu, this cynicism is nothing new. His entire political career has been built on managing the Palestinian question, not resolving it, and he has been happy to indirectly partner with Hamas to do so. In the years leading up to October 7, Netanyahu encouraged Qatar to fund Hamas in order to keep the leaders of the West Bank and Gaza divided and thus less capable of negotiating on Palestinians’ behalf. The logic that produced the policy remains essentially the same: whereas Netanyahu once needed a strong Hamas to prevent a peace process, he now needs only its specter.

The rest of the world, then, needs to stop being complicit in the fiction that Hamas is still a menace. U.S. and European governments must begin asking Israel to account for the gap between the threat it describes and the reality on the ground. Donor countries should condition reconstruction funding on Israeli compliance with the cease-fire’s provisions—which mandate a complete withdrawal of Israeli forces from Gaza, the opening of all border crossings, and the unrestricted return of displaced civilians—not on Hamas’s disarmament alone. Seeing through Netanyahu’s ploy is a prerequisite for rebuilding the lives of the two million Gazans left in the strip.

articles.title: Why Trump’s Congo Deal Is Falling Apart
articles.content:
2025年12月初，当刚果民主共和国和卢旺达的总统在华盛顿会面，为结束四年战火的和平协议举行签署仪式时，这本应展示美国总统唐纳德·特朗普解决最棘手冲突的能力。特朗普用他标志性的夸张言辞为签署仪式造势：“看看他们多么相爱，”他惊叹于两位总统的表现。但刚果的费利克斯·齐塞克迪和卢旺达的保罗·卡加梅拒绝握手。卢旺达军队已在南基伍省集结，准备发动新一轮重大攻势。几天之内，乌维拉镇——这是加丹加富矿地区以南150英里处最后一个主要城市中心——已落入卢旺达支持的M23叛军之手。

特朗普政府处理刚果的方式为其新的非洲战略提供了一个测试案例——这是一种交易性战略优先考虑达成交易、以政治支持换取自然资源协议，并威胁对外国政府实施制裁或扣留美国对外援助。过去18个月里，华盛顿在刚果获得了自冷战以来从未有过的影响力。它试图调解刚果东部的冲突；支持美国公司获取该国大量的钴、铜和锂储量；并帮助建设了非洲大陆最大的经济走廊之一。

特朗普政府押注可以利用其经济和政治影响力夺取刚果关键矿物的控制权，同时通过在东部强制实现和平来赢得声望。华盛顿在这两个目标上都取得了一些进展。但其对刚果的处理也展示了特朗普交易方法的局限性。他的政府依赖快速、宽泛的协议，将棘手细节的讨价还价推迟，这将使其更难实现长期目标——建立持久的地区稳定以及打破中国在关键矿物供应链上的主导地位。最令人担忧的是，“美国优先”政策可能倾向于与刚果政治领导人建立密切关系，而非关注民主和人权，这种做法可能最终导致该国不稳定。

除非政府调整其做法，否则刚果东部的和平进程面临崩溃风险，美国的矿业投资可能一无所获，而刚果总统可能加速其威权转向。刚果东部的冲突可追溯至30年前。在多轮涉及不同组合对抗者的战斗中，暴力的主轴一直是金沙萨政府和基加利政府之间的对抗。卢旺达军队于1996年首次入侵，追击帮助实施两年前卢旺达大屠杀的武装团体；此后，卢旺达政府在当地支持了四次叛乱。它声称受到刚果境内卢旺达叛军的威胁（事实上，自1996年以来该威胁已大幅下降），但其动机还包括建立缓冲带、开采自然资源以及管理军方潜在挑战和为压制国内异见辩护。

2021年底，长期自2013年战败后基本处于休眠状态的M23叛军组织开始在刚果东部发动新一轮攻击。这场持续至今的战争已使数百万人流离失所。旨在促进区域一体化的八国政府间组织东非共同体试图斡旋刚果冲突各方谈判，并部署了一支干预部队；安哥拉政府代表非洲联盟主持刚果和卢旺达之间的谈判；南部非洲发展共同体向刚果军队派遣了支援部队。所有这些努力都失败了，原因是刚果军队的失能和刚果、卢旺达两国政府的不配合。与此同时，欧盟一直无法就实施对卢旺达的广泛制裁或其他措施达成共识，主要原因是一些成员国渴望与卡加梅保持良好关系。

2024年末特朗普再次当选后，局势似乎可能加剧战斗。北基伍省首府戈马在特朗普第二届任期开始仅一周后就落入M23叛军之手，大多数观察人士怀疑卢旺达和M23有意选择这个时机发动进攻，以利用权力交接的空档期——届时关键政府官员尚未就位，无法做出快速果断的反应。然而，在职业外交官推动下（并在M23向一座美国所有的锡矿推进之后），特朗普政府开始介入，于2025年2月对卡加梅的一名关键顾问实施制裁，并于4月任命马萨德·布洛斯——特朗普女儿蒂芙尼的岳父——为非洲事务高级顾问。布洛斯是一名流利的法语使用者，在尼日利亚有商业经验，他迅速组织了刚果和卢旺达政府之间的谈判，而卡塔尔则主持了刚果政府与M23之间的谈判。布洛斯上任仅一周后的干预对于确保M23从锡矿周边地区撤军也至关重要——该矿后来被出售给一个阿联酋集团。

传统上，美国对刚果东部的政策一直通过由国家安全委员会领导的跨部门程序进行协调，涉及国务院非洲事务局、财政部、情报机构、美国国际开发署，在某些情况下还包括国务院其他部门和国防部，汇集了众多职业外交官和地区专家。特朗普政府绕过了这一程序。非洲政策的正式责任仍由国务卿马尔科·卢比奥领导的国务院负责，但关于刚果-卢旺达-M23危机的关键决策涉及的是布洛斯——他在被任命前没有任何政府工作经验。与美国中东和乌克兰特使一样，布洛斯的影响力与其说来自其机构职位，不如说来自对话者认为他与总统的密切关系，以及他在外交、商业和政治领域跨越运作的能力。

缺乏政策程序反而产生了一些前任美国政府未能取得的快速成果。外交官和财政部官员成功克服了五角大楼（与卢旺达军队关系密切）和卢旺达经济发展模式仰慕者对制裁卢旺达的典型阻力。在布洛斯上任仅几周后，刚果和卢旺达外长签署了一项联合声明，承诺结束战争。2025年6月，刚果-卢旺达和平协议签署（卡加梅和齐塞克迪同年12月在华盛顿仪式上重新签署），结束了敌对行动，解决了卢旺达的安全关切，并规定卢旺达军队撤离。2025年11月，卡塔尔斡旋的框架协议为刚果与M23之间的未来和平协议勾勒了轮廓。这些努力帮助停止了卢旺达在战场上的快速推进。

特朗普政府公开将经济利益与维和相结合，也最初有助于谈判。2025年12月，在监督和平协议签署的同时，特朗普政府促成了一项平行的区域经济一体化协议，提出美国资助两国边境地区的水电站、联合国家公园和旅游管理，以及可为两国带来收入的矿物供应链。华盛顿还与金沙萨签署了战略伙伴关系协议。该协议的条款要求刚果给予美国及其盟友——协议文本中称为“与美国立场一致的人员”——在某些关键矿物项目上优先竞标权。美国公司随后承诺在刚果矿业进行大量投资。在私人交谈中，刚果高级官员告诉我们，他们对美国的提议是“帮我们摆脱卢旺达，我们就给你们矿物。”

刚果迅速履行了其协议义务。协议签署后第二天，刚果国有矿业公司Gécamines和总部位于瑞士的能源及商品交易商Mercuria宣布成立合资企业（由美国国际开发金融公司支持，这是一个为推进美国利益而在发展中国家融资私营部门基础设施项目的政府机构），向美国及其盟国买家营销钴、铜和其他关键矿物。1月，Gécamines宣布将行使其权利，从滕科-丰古鲁梅矿购买10万吨铜出口到美国市场。其他交易接踵而至：总部位于纽约的猎户座关键矿物财团签署了一份不具约束力的谅解备忘录，拟收购嘉能可在刚果的铜钴资产40%的股份；Virtus Minerals——一家由前军方和情报官员创立的美国公司——收购了迪拜公司所有的矿业企业Chemaf的铜钴资产；加拿大公司艾芬豪矿业将自己定位为美国战略伙伴，正在与Gécamines和Mercuria进行深入谈判，从其在刚果的一座矿山向美国供应关键矿物。

这些举措——既有具体成果也有尚未实现的雄心——符合特朗普政府更广泛的非洲“美国优先”战略，该战略寻求改善美国对供应链的控制并推动美国公司在非洲大陆的发展。该战略的主要工具之一是开发金融公司。2025年，国会将其最大或有负债上限提高了三倍多，达到2050亿美元。它在安哥拉、加蓬、马拉维、卢旺达、赞比亚以及刚果购买了股权并提供了贷款和贷款担保。它是洛比托走廊的主要资助者之一——该项目旨在现代化安哥拉的港口基础设施、公路和800英里铁路，将刚果和赞比亚的铜钴带与连接到大西洋，可能减少对中国人主导的物流和加工网络的依赖。在西非，类似的项目“自由走廊”也在争取美国政府的支持。

华盛顿在和平谈判中对卢旺达政府也成功施加了重大压力，最近对卢旺达国防军及其几名高级军官、前刚果总统约瑟夫·卡比拉（金沙萨指控他援助M23）以及一家政府所有的黄金精炼厂实施制裁。作为回应，M23和卢旺达军队已从一些地区撤军。然而，没有总体战略，仅靠压力不太可能阻止冲突。卢旺达的撤军看起来主要是战术性的，M23和刚果军队之间的战斗仍在继续。刚果军队（在私营军事公司的协助下）和M23都开始更依赖无人机，这些无人机也在杀害平民。3月，金沙萨授权的一次无人机袭击在戈马误杀了一名联合国儿童基金会的人道主义工作者。

任何和平协议都需要双方做出妥协。卢旺达及其M23盟友——在大多数地区军事上优于刚果军队——不太可能在战争损失数百名士兵后放弃相当于马里兰州大小的领土控制权。然而，这正是刚果政府目前的立场——它要求卢旺达在谈判开始前放下武器。面对这种极端主义立场，卢旺达可能会坚持立场并等待——等待特朗普离任或等待新的危机席卷金沙萨。华府提供的经济激励似乎也未能说服卢旺达放弃其在刚果东部追求长期安全、经济和政治利益的努力。相反，特朗普对该地区美国经济利益的关注导致了一场不对称的施压运动——华盛顿努力管控卢旺达的行为，却未对刚果施加同等力度的措施。这种不平衡可能鼓励金沙萨拒绝妥协，这将破坏持久和平所需的大交易，并创造刚果总统攫取更多权力、削弱民主制度的威权倾向的条件。

最后一种危险已变得越来越明显。2024年10月，齐塞克迪开始暗示他有意修改或替换刚果宪法，可能为了寻求第三个任期。自华盛顿最近一轮对基加利的制裁以来，齐塞克迪的联盟更加积极地推动宪法变革，镇压反对派成员和批评者。



齐塞克迪政府官员在私下交谈中毫不讳言，他们相信特朗普会以接受齐塞克迪第三次连任为条件，换取刚果矿产的准入权及其他好处，比如同意接收被美国驱逐的移民。（今年4月，第一批此类移民已抵达金沙萨。）

特朗普政府已明确表示对推广民主或人权缺乏兴趣，但将赌注押在刚果独裁统治上作为稳定的保障，将损害本届政府在谈判中优先考虑的经济利益。不负责任的领导和金沙萨的劣政将助长对政府的反对，在刚果安全部门本就薄弱且分裂的情况下尤其危险。美国对齐塞克迪的青睐会传递出这样的信息：唯一能让他下台的方式是通过法外手段，这将迫使温和的反对派领导人与武装叛乱组织结盟或加入其中。

更何况，政府的做法能否为美国带来可观收益尚不明朗。华盛顿对非洲矿产的兴趣旨在减少对中国的依赖，但中国企业在刚果的工业、小规模和个体采矿业中已占据主导地位。即使美国存在大幅扩张，也不太可能取代中国在非洲的供应链。超过三分之二的刚果铜和钴出口流向中国；超过一半的非洲铝土矿、锰、钽和钨也走同样的路线。由于工业产能高度集中在中国，供应链的下一环节——高级精炼和制造业——同样被中国企业主导。此外，特朗普政府杂乱无章的外交方式可能最终损害美国在该地区的影响力。国务院的精简和弱化仍在持续，对美国政策制定造成了严重后果。在本届政府执政的前16个月里，非洲事务局由一系列代理负责人领导，华盛顿在非洲52个大使馆中的39个没有派驻大使，其中包括刚果和卢旺达的大使馆。这种能力的缺失已经削弱了美国进行复杂外交的能力，导致政策缺乏连贯性。例如，自卢旺达在2025年底加大对刚果的干涉以来，华盛顿宣布向卢旺达提供高达1.58亿美元的医疗援助计划，并签署了民用核能战略合作备忘录，这破坏了其对基加利实施制裁所施加的压力。

打破历届美国政府对非政策的模式本身并非坏事。约束卢旺达必须是解决M23危机的第一步；在这一点上，特朗普政府取得了一些进展。但特朗普做法的弊端现在开始压过其成果。像美国这样的超级大国的临时性、不可预测的外交政策可能产生快速成效，但随着时间推移，各方会进行调整和重新校准；已经在基加利的一些西方外交官怀疑，卢旺达可能只是在等待特朗普离任或失去兴趣。即使两国达成和平协议，冲突的复杂性和根深蒂固的性质——涉及数十个武装组织——仍然需要一项全面、长期的和平计划。美国在刚果开展商业外交的时间框架是数年和数十年，而不是数月，美国必须进行更长期的游戏。

主要向卢旺达施压而与刚果套近乎不会结束战争。相反，华盛顿应该促使刚果停止寻求军事解决方案，在与M23的谈判中做出妥协，并尊重现行宪法的任期限制。白宫可以利用制裁、签证限制和高层次政治信息来推动可持续和平并保护刚果的民主——包括明确表示反对齐塞克迪的第三个任期，或任何让他在2028年任期结束后继续执政的企图。

目前，尽管有众多仪式、声明和拍照机会，刚果的冲突仍然陷入僵局。没有一个交战国有立即妥协的兴趣，而华盛顿在平衡其调解者和经济利益方的双重角色方面已开始达到极限。通过不必要地激怒美国的传统伙伴，包括欧洲国家、南非和联合国，华盛顿使得协调政策变得更加困难——而这种协调在过去推动和平进程方面曾发挥作用。最坏的情况是，美国在刚果的政策冻结了M23危机，同时刚果走向越来越独裁，在稀土供应链多元化远离中国方面几乎无所作为。没有长期战略目标的短期胜利很快就会感觉像是一场彻底的失败。
articles.summary:
When the presidents of the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Rwanda met in Washington, D.C., in early December 2025, for a ceremony sealing the peace deal that ended four years of fighting, it was meant to showcase U.S. President Donald Trump’s ability to settle even the most intractable conflicts. Trump blanketed the signing event with his trademark hyperbole: “Look at how they love each other,” he marveled about the two presidents. But Congo’s Felix Tshisekedi and Rwanda’s Paul Kagame refused to shake hands. Rwandan troops were already massing in South Kivu Province to launch a major new offensive. Within days, the South Kivu town of Uvira—the last urban center north of the resource-rich Katanga region, 150 miles to the south—had fallen to the Rwanda-backed M23 rebel group.

The Trump administration’s approach to Congo has offered a test case for its new Africa strategy—a transactional one that prioritizes dealmaking, trades political support for natural resource deals, and threatens foreign governments with sanctions or the withholding of U.S. foreign assistance. Over the past 18 months, Washington has taken on a prominence in Congo not seen since the Cold War. It has sought to mediate the conflict in eastern Congo; supported U.S. companies seeking access to the country’s substantial cobalt, copper, and lithium reserves; and helped build one of the largest economic corridors on the continent. The Trump administration is betting it can use its economic and political leverage to wrest control of Congolese critical minerals while gaining prestige by imposing peace in the east.

Washington has made some progress toward both of these aims. But its handling of Congo is also a demonstration of the limits of Trump’s transactional method. His administration’s reliance on swift, broad agreements that postpone haggling over tough details will make it harder to achieve its long-term goals of establishing lasting regional stability and disrupting China’s dominance over critical minerals supply chains. Most worryingly, an “America first” policy is likely to favor close relations with Congolese political leaders over democracy and human rights, an approach that could end up destabilizing the country. Unless the administration adjusts its approach, the peace process in eastern Congo is at risk of collapse, U.S. mining ventures could amount to little, and the Congolese president could accelerate his authoritarian turn.

PEACE DIVIDEND?

Conflict in eastern Congo stretches back 30 years. Over the course of many rounds of fighting involving different permutations of antagonists, the main axis of violence has been between the governments in Kinshasa and Kigali. The Rwandan army invaded for the first time in 1996, in pursuit of armed groups that helped perpetrate the Rwandan genocide two years prior; since then, the Rwandan government has backed four rebellions in the area. It cites the threat of Congo-based Rwandan rebels (which, in fact, has declined dramatically since 1996), but its motivations also include the establishment of a buffer zone, the extraction of natural resources, and the need to manage potential challenges by its own army and justify suppression of domestic dissent.

In late 2021, the M23 rebel group, which had been largely dormant since its defeat in 2013, began a new series of attacks in eastern Congo. The ensuing war has displaced several million people. The East African Community, an eight-country intergovernmental organization promoting regional integration, attempted to broker talks between Congolese belligerents and deployed an intervention force; the Angolan government hosted talks between Congo and Rwanda on behalf of the African Union; and the Southern African Development Community sent troops to back the Congolese army. All these initiatives failed, stymied by the dysfunction of the Congolese army and the recalcitrance of the Congolese and Rwandan governments. The European Union, meanwhile, has been unable to find consensus to implement broad-ranging sanctions or other measures against Rwanda, largely due to some members’ eagerness to maintain good relations with Kagame.

It seemed that Trump’s reelection, in late 2024, might exacerbate the fighting. Goma, the capital of North Kivu Province, fell to M23 rebels just a week after Trump’s second term began, and most observers suspected that Rwanda and the M23 had timed the assault to take advantage of the interregnum, when key administration officials would not yet be in place to enable a quick and decisive reaction. With a push from career diplomats (and after an M23 advance on a U.S.-owned tin mine), however, the Trump administration began to intervene, sanctioning a key adviser to Kagame in February 2025 and naming Massad Boulos, the father-in-law of Trump’s daughter Tiffany, as his senior adviser for Africa in April. A fluent French speaker with business experience in Nigeria, Boulos quickly organized talks between the Congolese and Rwandan governments, while Qatar hosted negotiations between the Congolese government and the M23. Boulos’s intervention, just a week after his appointment, was also crucial in securing the withdrawal of M23 forces from the area surrounding the tin mine, which was later sold to an Emirati group.

Traditionally, U.S. policy toward eastern Congo had been coordinated through an interagency process led by the National Security Council and involving the State Department’s Africa Bureau, the Treasury Department, intelligence agencies, the U.S. Agency for International Development, and, in some cases, other parts of the State Department as well as the Department of Defense, incorporating a wide variety of career diplomats and regional experts. The Trump administration has short-circuited this process. Formal responsibility for Africa policy remains within the State Department under Secretary of State Marco Rubio, but key decisions on the Congolese-Rwandan-M23 crisis involve Boulos, who had no government experience before his appointment. As with U.S. envoys to the Middle East and Ukraine, Boulos’s influence derives less from his institutional position than from what interlocutors perceive as his intimate relationship with the president and his ability to operate across diplomatic, commercial, and political domains.

It seemed that Trump’s reelection might exacerbate the fighting.

The lack of policy process produced some quick results that previous U.S. administrations had failed to achieve. Diplomats and Treasury officials were able to overcome the typical pushback against sanctions on Rwanda from the Pentagon (which has strong ties with the Rwandan army) and admirers of Rwanda’s economic development. And just weeks after Boulos’s appointment, the Congolese and Rwandan foreign ministers signed a joint declaration pledging their intent to end the war. In June 2025, a Congolese-Rwandan peace agreement was signed (the same agreement Kagame and Tshisekedi ceremonially re-signed in December in Washington), ending hostilities, addressing Rwanda’s security concerns, and mandating the withdrawal of Rwandan forces. November 2025 brought a Qatari-brokered framework agreement outlining a future peace deal between Congo and the M23. These efforts helped bring an end to Rwanda’s rapid advances on the battlefield.

The Trump administration’s overt mixing of economic interests with peacemaking also initially helped the negotiations. In December 2025, alongside overseeing the signing of the peace deal, the Trump administration brokered a parallel regional economic integration agreement that proposed U.S. funding for a hydroelectric plant on the border between the two countries, joint national parks and tourism management, and a mineral supply chain that could provide revenue for both countries. Washington also signed a strategic partnership agreement with Kinshasa. The terms of the agreement required Congo to give the United States and its allies—“U.S. aligned persons” in the agreement’s text—the right to bid first on certain critical minerals projects. U.S. companies then pledged to make large investments in the Congolese mining sector. In private conversations, senior Congolese officials told us their pitch to the Americans was “Get Rwanda off our back, and we will give you minerals.”

Congo quickly delivered on its end of the deal. The day after the agreement was signed, the Congolese mining parastatal Gécamines and the Swiss-based energy and commodity trader Mercuria announced a joint venture (backed by the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation, a government agency that finances private-sector infrastructure projects in developing nations to advance American interests) to market cobalt, copper, and other critical minerals to the United States and its allied buyers. In January, Gécamines announced that it would exercise its right to purchase 100,000 tons of copper from the Tenke Fungurume mine for export to the U.S. market. Other deals followed: the New York–based Orion Critical Mineral Consortium signed a nonbinding memorandum of understanding to acquire a 40 percent stake in Glencore’s Congolese copper and cobalt assets; Virtus Minerals, a U.S.-based firm founded by former military and intelligence officials, moved to take over the copper and cobalt assets of Chemaf, a mining enterprise owned by a Dubai-based company; and Ivanhoe Mines, a Canadian company presenting itself as a U.S. strategic partner, entered advanced talks with Gécamines and Mercuria to supply the United States with critical minerals from one of its Congolese mines.

These moves, a mix of concrete wins and as-yet-unfulfilled ambition, fit into the Trump administration’s broader “America first” strategy in Africa, which seeks to improve U.S. control over supply chains and boost U.S. companies on the continent. One of the main tools of this strategy is the Development Finance Corporation. In 2025, Congress expanded the agency’s maximum contingent liability cap more than threefold, to $205 billion. It has bought equity in and given loans and loan guarantees to projects in Angola, Gabon, Malawi, Rwanda, and Zambia, as well as Congo. It is one of the main funders of the Lobito Corridor, a project to modernize port infrastructure, roads, and 800 miles of railroad within Angola to connect the copper and cobalt belts of Congo and Zambia to the Atlantic Ocean, potentially reducing dependence on Chinese-dominated logistics and processing networks. In West Africa, a similar project, the Liberty Corridor, is also trying to attract U.S. government support.

SOFT-BALL DIPLOMACY

Washington has also successfully applied significant pressure on the Rwandan government as part of peace negotiations, recently levying sanctions on the Rwanda Defence Force and several of its top officers, former Congolese President Joseph Kabila (accused by Kinshasa of aiding the M23), and a government-owned gold refinery. In response, the M23 and the Rwandan army have withdrawn troops from some areas. Without an overarching strategy, however, pressure alone is unlikely to work to stop the conflict. Rwanda’s withdrawals appear largely tactical, and fighting has continued between the M23 and the Congolese army. The Congolese army, assisted by private military companies, and the M23 have begun to rely more on drones, which are also killing civilians. In March, a drone attack in Goma, authorized by Kinshasa, mistakenly killed a UNICEF humanitarian worker.

Any peace deal will require compromises from both sides. Rwanda and its M23 allies—militarily superior to Congolese forces in most areas—are unlikely to cede control of a territory the size of Maryland after having lost hundreds of troops in the war. And yet this is the current stance of the Congolese government, which demands that Rwanda lay down its weapons before talks can begin. Faced with this maximalism, Rwanda is likely to dig in and wait—for Trump to leave or for a new crisis to engulf Kinshasa. The economic incentives on offer from Washington also appear to have failed to convince Rwanda to give up its pursuit of long-standing security, economic, and political interests in eastern Congo.

Instead, Trump’s focus on U.S. economic interests in the region has resulted in a lopsided pressure campaign in which Washington has worked to police Rwandan behavior without applying equally forceful measures on the Congolese. This imbalance could encourage Kinshasa to refuse to compromise, which would undermine the grand bargain required for lasting peace and create the conditions for an authoritarian drift in which the Congolese president grabs more power for himself and weakens democratic institutions.

That last danger has become increasingly apparent. In October 2024, Tshisekedi began signaling his interest in revising or replacing the Congolese constitution, potentially to run for a third term. Since Washington’s latest round of sanctions on Kigali, Tshisekedi’s coalition has even more aggressively pursued constitutional change, cracking down on opposition members and critics. In private conversations, members of Tshisekedi’s administration have not been coy about their conviction that Trump will accept a third Tshisekedi term in return for access to Congolese minerals and other favors, such as agreeing to host migrants who have been expelled from the United States. (A first wave of such migrants arrived in Kinshasa in April.)

The Trump administration has made it clear that it has little interest in promoting democracy or human rights, but banking on Congolese authoritarianism as a guarantor of stability will undermine the economic interests the administration has prioritized in negotiations. Unaccountable leadership and bad governance in Kinshasa will embolden opposition to the government, an especially dangerous prospect given how weak and fractious Congo’s security services are. A U.S. embrace of Tshisekedi would send the message that the only way to remove him is through extralegal means, pushing moderate opposition leaders to ally with or join armed rebel groups.

It is not even clear that the administration’s approach will yield significant returns for the United States. Washington is interested in African minerals to reduce dependence on China, whose companies already dominate industrial, small-scale, and artisanal mining in Congo. But even a huge expansion in U.S. presence is unlikely to displace Chinese supply chains in Africa. Over two-thirds of Congo’s copper and cobalt exports go to China; over half the continent’s bauxite, manganese, tantalum, and tungsten follow the same route. Because industrial capacity is overwhelmingly concentrated in China, the next part of the supply chain—higher-level refining and manufacturing—is also dominated by companies based there.

Moreover, the Trump administration’s scattershot diplomatic approach may ultimately hurt U.S. power in the region. The gutting and weakening of the State Department continues apace, with harsh consequences for U.S. policymaking. The Africa Bureau was led by a succession of acting heads for the first 16 months of the administration, and Washington does not have an ambassador in 39 of its 52 embassies in Africa, including those in Congo and Rwanda.

This lack of capacity has already undermined the country’s ability to conduct complex diplomacy, leading to incoherent policy. Since Rwanda escalated its meddling in Congo in late 2025, for example, Washington has announced a plan to provide Rwanda up to $158 million for health-care assistance and signed a memorandum on strategic civil nuclear cooperation, undermining the pressure it has put on Kigali with sanctions.

THE LONG GAME

Breaking the mold of past administrations’ dealings with Africa is not a bad thing in itself. Reining in Rwanda had to be the first step toward resolving the M23 crisis; on this, the Trump administration has made some progress. But the disadvantages of Trump’s approach are now starting to overwhelm its victories. Ad hoc, unpredictable foreign policy by a superpower like the United States can produce quick results, but over time, however, actors adjust and recalibrate; already, some Western diplomats in Kigali suspect that Rwanda may simply weather the punishment from Washington while waiting for Trump to leave office or lose interest. Even if there is a peace deal between the two countries, the complexity and entrenched nature of the conflict, which includes dozens of armed groups, will still require a comprehensive, long-term peace plan.

The time frame for U.S. commercial diplomacy to flourish in Congo is years and decades, not months, and the United States must play a longer game. Primarily pressuring Rwanda while cozying up to Congo will not end the war. Washington should instead impel Congo to stop seeking a military solution to the conflict, to make compromises in negotiations with the M23, and to respect the current constitution’s term limits. The White House can use sanctions, visa restrictions, and high-level political messaging to push for a sustainable peace and protect Congo’s democracy—including clearly stating that it opposes a third term for Tshisekedi or any other attempts by him to remain in power beyond the end of his term, in 2028.

For now, despite the many ceremonies, declarations, and photo ops, the conflict in Congo remains deadlocked. None of the belligerents have an immediate interest in compromise, and Washington’s approach to balancing its roles as mediator and economically interested party has begun to reach its limits. And by needlessly antagonizing the United States’ traditional partners, including European countries, South Africa, and the United Nations, Washington has made it even more difficult to coordinate policy in ways that have been useful in advancing past peace processes. The worst-case scenario is that U.S. policy in Congo freezes the M23 crisis while Congo turns increasingly authoritarian, and little is accomplished in diversifying mineral supply chains away from China. A short-term victory without a long-term strategic goal will soon feel like no victory at all.

articles.title: How to Save the U.S.-Israeli Alliance
articles.content:
2015年，当美国与伊朗谈判《联合全面行动计划》（JCPOA）——一份以色列并非缔约方的协议时——我们在《外交事务》上撰文主张，以色列和美国应达成一项平行协议，确保美伊任何协议都能持久并符合以色列利益。以色列和美国拥有相同的目标——阻止伊朗获得核武器——但在威胁认知、历史创伤、时间节点以及对制裁、秘密行动和空袭等外交替代方案的容忍度上存在分歧。平行协议有助于建立统一阵线，并在伊朗越过任何红线时协调应对。然而，以色列和美国最终并未达成自己的协议。后果是严重的。JCPOA成为美以两国政府之间摩擦的根源，并让以色列和海湾阿拉伯国家的人民感到美国轻视了他们的安全。该协议从签署之日起就政治脆弱。它暂时限制了伊朗核计划的部分内容，但未能阻止德黑兰的弹道导弹建设或其对代理人的支持。关键条款仅在几年后就将到期。而且，以色列和美国从未共同为伊朗可能停止遵守该协议的可能性做准备，而伊朗最终在美国于2018年单方面退出该协议后确实这么做了。总之，围绕JCPOA缺乏以色列的参与，为十年后伊朗、以色列和美国走上冲突之路创造了条件。在许多方面，当前时刻与2015年核协议之前的准备阶段如出一辙。德黑兰和华盛顿再次进行核谈判。美国再次面临以相对较少的让步换取伊朗大量救济的风险——这令以色列和某些海湾国家大为不满。美伊谅解备忘录重新开放霍尔木兹海峡、稳定能源市场、将战争暂停60天，并创建了保持谈判的外交渠道。然而，它并未解决引发危机的核心分歧：伊朗的核野心、导弹计划和对代理人的支持。因此，谈判可能在加强德黑兰手中筹码的同时冻结战场。毕竟，谅解备忘录赋予了伊朗重建所需的时间、资金和合法性，同时将真正解决方案推迟到将来。因此，以色列和美国现在必须做他们2015年未能做到的事情：达成一致。这意味着他们需要就什么是可接受的核协议达成自己的协议，并协调应对措施，以防与伊朗的谈判未能产生协议。否则，两国为打击伊朗及其代理人所投入的鲜血、财富和声望可能都将付诸东流。

从一开始，以色列和美国就对伊朗有着不同的战争目标。对以色列而言，伊朗的威胁是生存性的。该国不会接受一个拥有核能力、拥有弹道导弹防御盾牌、并由一个公开呼吁毁灭以色列的政权控制着地区火环的伊朗。以色列对伊朗的战争是防御性的，旨在防止2023年10月7日哈马斯在以色列杀害1200人并绑架200多人事件的重演，甚至防止上世纪四十年代初欧洲大屠杀的重演。在以色列看来，确保自身安全的唯一方法是更换伊朗政权，建立一个对以色列不怀敌意、愿意放弃伊朗核野心以及在该地区破坏稳定努力的政府。在美以于2025年6月对伊朗发动打击、伊朗政权被削弱、2026年1月爆发大规模抗议之后，许多以色列领导人得出结论，德黑兰可能发生更深入的政治变革。到美以于2025年2月下旬开始对伊朗发动空袭时，以色列的战争目标已是政权更迭，尽管政府并未公开承认。华盛顿采取了更为谨慎的方针，从未投入实现政权更迭所需的资源。尽管以色列愿意承受长期海湾战争的经济负担，但特朗普政府不愿意。华盛顿还犯下了一系列使胜利变得不可能的错误。特朗普政府未能为霍尔木兹海峡关闭制定计划，也从未对伊朗针对海湾关键基础设施的威胁制定充分的应对措施。它未能向普通美国人传达战争的目标以及实现这些目标所需的资源。最后，政府未能认识到黎巴嫩可以被用作对德黑兰施压的核心手段，而是将其视为一个单独的问题。因此，通过限制以色列对真主党的回应，美国剥夺了自己对伊朗的杠杆。如果华盛顿让以色列扮演强硬角色，本会更好——保留美国的外交灵活性，同时明确告知德黑兰，通过真主党升级将带来直接代价。然而，最终以色列过于雄心勃勃的目标与特朗普政府对打击一个顽强对手的冷淡承诺并不匹配。新伊朗政权准备承受巨大痛苦，并证明自己能让美国付出高昂的经济代价。华盛顿因此做出让步，伊朗政权则变得更加大胆。事实上，伊朗精英阶层中一个更加激进和复仇心更强的派系，似乎已在德黑兰掌权，这个派系由伊斯兰革命卫队主导。以色列政府则对仅凭空中力量就能催化政权更迭的理念寄予了过多信任。在此过程中，它忽视了一个历史教训：政权只能通过占领（如二战后的德国和2003年的伊拉克）或通过人民起义来实现成功更迭。

目前，以色列和华盛顿的核心任务是防止谅解备忘录建立的60天窗口期成为德黑兰的恢复期。这首先要从阻止伊朗控制霍尔木兹海峡开始。因此，美国应在海湾保持大量海军存在，最好是在由地区和欧洲伙伴组成的广泛联盟下，以阻止对航运的干扰。该联盟还必须扩大情报收集、海上巡逻和导弹防御覆盖范围，确保与伊朗结盟的也门胡塞武装不会关闭连接红海和印度洋的曼德海峡。同时，美国及其盟友应利用霍尔木兹海峡暂时开放的机会补充石油储备、恢复军事库存，并加强海湾安全基础设施以抵御导弹、无人机和网络攻击。华盛顿应迅速建立一个永久性区域防御体系，使美国在该地区的合作伙伴在美国中央司令部的领导下整合各自的预警系统，最终发展成为一个能够有效对抗伊朗导弹和无人机威胁的综合防空架构。美国在该地区的合作伙伴还应协调能源基础设施的网络防御。

在防止伊朗对世界其他地区施加经济压力的同时，以色列和美国必须保持对伊朗的经济压力。德黑兰对资金的迫切需求是一种不应被浪费的杠杆。因此，任何未来的制裁减免都应是渐进的、可逆的，并以伊朗可核查的让步为条件。伊朗不应仅凭模糊承诺就获得大笔款项。如果释放的资金被用于导弹、代理部队、国内镇压或伊斯兰革命卫队网络，那只会为下一轮战斗提供补贴。目前，谅解备忘录中的一项条款允许伊朗重新获得部分被冻结资产；这应该缩小范围，并以伊朗全面履行其在谅解备忘录下的承诺为条件。只要伊朗仍然违反这些承诺，特朗普政府就必须坚持要求该地区各地的银行、企业和个人停止向伊朗转账。为了确保这种压力产生最大效果，以色列和美国需要加强合作。两国应立即恢复高级别跨部门工作组，以便在领导人公开争论之前敲定下一阶段停火的细节。这些工作组应专注于保持对伊朗的可信军事威胁以防止挑衅、建立联合情报机制以侦测和阻止伊朗核计划和导弹计划的重建、协调制裁执行，以及制定保卫海湾基础设施和海上航线的共同计划。以色列和美国在某些问题上将继续存在分歧。通过提前识别这些领域，它们可以保持对伊朗的压力，避免公开裂痕，并剥夺德黑兰利用它们之间差距的机会。美国和以色列领导人可以通过签署一项框架来推进它们的共同目标——防止核伊朗的崛起和伊朗地区轴心的复苏——该框架定义它们在60天窗口期间及之后将采取的行动。该框架应包括一项旨在侦测伊朗核计划任何进展的情报行动，结合以色列在执行行动和培养人力情报方面的卓越能力与美国在卫星和金融情报方面的优势。以色列和美国可以同意建立一个结构化流程来评估伊朗的决策、制裁规避以及核和导弹活动。这也将有助于确保情报在政治压力或军事升级期间得到专业和一致的评估。当违规证据出现时，两国应能够迅速评估并协调政策回应。任何美以平行协议还应包括一个“如果-那么”矩阵，双方提前同意如何处理伊朗的违规行为。例如，如果伊朗阻止核检查员、将浓缩材料转移到军事保管下，或重建加固核设施，美国应重新实施制裁。如果伊朗代理人攻击以色列、美国军队、海湾能源基础设施或国际航运，以色列和美国不仅应打击代理人，还应打击此次攻击背后的伊朗指挥、后勤和资金架构。美以协议不会是给以色列的恩惠。在美以协议下，两国还应确立它们可以接受的伊朗核协议的参数。以色列不应原则上拒绝与伊朗的外交；它只应反对那些让德黑兰拥有重建核计划所需能力、资源或时间的协议。美国应保证只签署包含以下内容的伊朗核协议：移除伊朗所有浓缩材料、零浓缩能力、强化检查、无日落条款，以及对可疑地点的快速进入机制。这些标准确实要求苛刻，德黑兰长期以来一直抵制，但它们是必要的。没有美国对以色列军事援助的长期承诺，美以协议就不完整。以色列迫切需要补充弹药、空中和导弹防御能力、远程打击能力，以及美国预置的库存。过去几个月来，以色列总理内塔尼亚胡表示，他计划确保他的国家不再依赖美国的援助。但他现在应该重新考虑这一方针。恢复以色列的军事能力将使美国能够减少自身在该地区的存在，而不会削弱对伊朗的威慑或美国的外交努力。但这并不意味着华盛顿提供的援助类型应该保持不变。事实上，从长远来看，美国的军事援助应该转向联合研发和生产先进武器，包括下一代导弹防御拦截器，可能在特朗普的“金穹”计划下进行。更为重要的是建立技术联盟的共同投资需求，以保持美国在人工智能、量子计算、能源、半导体和关键材料方面的优势。这一联盟应在美国-以色列谅解备忘录下建立，在两国之间创造一个可信的生态系统，深化它们私营部门、大学和研究机构之间的合作。



这将产生一种新型的伙伴关系模式：不是建立在恩惠关系之上，而是建立在各国共同力量和联盟纽带之上的模式。通过协议与美国结盟符合美国的利益。这场战争展示了美国从以色列的知识、基础设施和作战能力中获得的价值。一个强大的以色列降低了美国自身必须使用武力来追求其地区目标的可能性。因此，美以协议对以色列来说不是一种恩惠，而是维护战争成果、分担执行与德黑兰协议负担以及提高美国在该地区承诺可信度的方式。如果不加以制止，伊朗将利用其备忘录规定的60天窗口期，利用以色列与美国之间的分歧，重建威胁该地区所需的能力——这将破坏伊朗战争最重要的成果。以色列始终偏好与华盛顿合作，但鉴于伊朗威胁的规模，以色列不能依赖盟友来保卫自己的公民。一份糟糕的美伊协议不会自动意味着战争。然而，如果伊朗追求核武器，或者真主党重建其军事基础设施或返回以色列边境，以色列将被迫独自行动。无论是否有美国参与，以色列的情报机构都需要继续渗透伊朗最高层。它需要保持从远方打击该国的能力，扩大导弹防御和民用韧性，悄悄深化与海湾国家的协调，并揭露伊朗的制裁规避行为。如果以色列的红线即将被逾越，它有权使用军事力量进行自卫。对以色列来说，单独行动将更加困难和危险。但这总比什么都不做强。这个国家绝不能允许外交成为伊朗重建威胁以色列生存能力的掩护。
articles.summary:
In 2015, as the United States negotiated the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA) with Iran—an agreement Israel was not a party to—we argued in Foreign Affairs that Israel and the United States should pursue a parallel agreement to ensure that any U.S.-Iranian deal would be durable and aligned with Israeli interests. Israel and the United States shared the same goal—preventing Iran from acquiring nuclear weapons—but differed over threat perceptions, historical trauma, timing, and tolerance for alternatives to diplomacy, such as sanctions, covert action, and airstrikes. A parallel agreement would help create a unified front and coordinate responses if Iran crossed any redlines.

In the end, however, Israel and the United States never made a pact of their own. The consequences were severe. The JCPOA became a source of friction between the U.S. and Israeli governments and created the sense among people in Israel and Gulf Arab countries that the United States had discounted their security. It was politically vulnerable from the moment it was signed.

The deal temporarily restricted parts of Iran’s nuclear program, but it did not stop Tehran’s ballistic missile buildup or its support for proxies. Key provisions were set to expire after just a few years. And Israel and the United States never jointly prepared for the possibility that Iran would stop complying with it, which Tehran eventually did after the United States unilaterally withdrew from the deal in 2018. Altogether, the lack of Israeli buy-in around the JCPOA created the conditions that put Iran, Israel, and the United States on a collision course a decade later.

In many ways, the current moment resembles the lead-up to the 2015 nuclear deal. Once again, Tehran and Washington are engaged in nuclear talks. And once again, the United States is at risk of offering Iran great relief in exchange for relatively little—much to the chagrin of Israel and certain Gulf countries. The U.S.-Iranian memorandum of understanding reopens the Strait of Hormuz, stabilizes energy markets, suspends the war for another 60 days, and creates a diplomatic channel to keep negotiating. Yet it does not resolve the core disagreements that produced the crisis in the first place: Iran’s nuclear ambitions, missile programs, and support for proxies. The negotiations could thus freeze the battlefield while strengthening Tehran’s hand. The memorandum, after all, grants Iran the time, money, and legitimacy needed to reconstitute while deferring real resolution for another day.

Israel and the United States must therefore do now what they failed to do in 2015: get on the same page. That means they need to forge their own deal about what an acceptable nuclear agreement would look like and coordinate their responses, should negotiations with Iran fail to produce an agreement. Otherwise, the blood, treasure, and prestige both countries have spent fighting Iran and its proxies might all be for naught.

BAD COP, RELUCTANT COP

From the start, Israel and the United States have had different Iran war aims. For Israel, the Iranian threat is existential. The country will not accept an Iran that has nuclear capabilities, a shield of ballistic missiles, and a regional ring of fire controlled by a regime that openly calls for Israel’s destruction. Israel’s war against Iran was meant defensively, to prevent a repeat of the slaughter on October 7, 2023, when Hamas killed 1,200 people in Israel and took 200 more hostage, or even a repeat of the Holocaust in Europe in the early 1940s. In Israel’s view, the only way to guarantee its safety was to replace the Iranian regime with a government that was not hostile to Israel and would give up Iran’s nuclear ambitions as well as its efforts to destabilize the region. After the Iranian regime was weakened by U.S.-Israeli strikes in June 2025 and by mass protests in January 2026, many Israeli leaders concluded that a deeper political transformation in Tehran was possible. By the time the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran began in late February, Israel’s goal for the war was regime change, even if the government did not say so publicly.

Washington took a less audacious approach to the war and never committed the resources that it would take to foment regime change. Although Israel was willing to absorb the economic burden of a prolonged Gulf war, the Trump administration was not. Washington also made a series of errors that made victory unreachable. The Trump administration failed to plan for the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and never developed an adequate response to Iranian threats against critical infrastructure in the Gulf. It failed to communicate to ordinary Americans the war’s objectives and the amount of resources it would take to achieve them. Finally, the administration failed to recognize that Lebanon could be used as a central means of pressure against Tehran, treating it instead as a separate issue. By restraining Israel’s response to Hezbollah, the United States therefore deprived itself of leverage over Iran. It would have been better for Washington to let Israel play the bad cop, preserving American diplomatic flexibility while making clear to Tehran that escalation through Hezbollah would carry direct costs.

In the end, however, Israel’s overly ambitious objectives did not match the Trump administration’s lukewarm commitment to fighting a tenacious rival. The new Iranian regime was ready to suffer enormous pain and proved it could impose a steep economic cost on the United States. Washington thus conceded, and Iran’s regime emerged emboldened. In fact, it appears that a more radical and vengeful faction of the country’s elite, dominated by the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), has taken the reins in Tehran. The Israeli government, for its part, placed too much faith in the idea that airpower alone could catalyze regime change. In doing so, it ignored the historical lesson that regimes can be successfully changed only through occupation—as in Germany after World War II and in Iraq in 2003—or by an uprising of the people.

KEEP UP THE PRESSURE

Right now, the central task for Israel and Washington is to prevent the 60-day window established by the memorandum from becoming a period of recovery for Tehran. That begins with denying Iran control over the Strait of Hormuz. The United States should therefore maintain a large naval presence in the Gulf, preferably under a broad coalition of regional and European partners, to stop disruptions to shipping. The coalition must also expand intelligence collection, maritime patrols, and missile defense coverage to ensure that the Houthi militia in Yemen, which is allied with Iran, doesn’t close the Bab el Mandeb Strait that connects the Red Sea to the Indian Ocean.

At the same time, the United States and its allies should use the temporary opening of the Strait of Hormuz to replenish oil reserves, restore military stockpiles, and harden Gulf security infrastructure against missiles, drones, and cyberattacks. Washington should quickly establish a permanent regional defense system in which U.S. partners in the region integrate their early warning systems with one another under U.S. Central Command, eventually developing into an effective integrated air-defense architecture capable of countering Iranian missile and drone threats. U.S. partners in the region should also coordinate the cyberdefense of energy infrastructure.

As they prevent Iran from putting economic pressure on the rest of the world, Israel and the United States must keep economic pressure on Iran. Tehran’s desperate need for money is a source of leverage that should not be squandered. Any future sanctions relief should therefore be gradual, reversible, and conditioned on verifiable Iranian concessions. Iran should not receive major payouts in exchange for vague promises. If released funds are used for missiles, proxy forces, internal repression, or IRGC networks, they will have merely subsidized the next round of fighting. Currently, a provision in the memorandum of understanding allows Iran renewed access to some frozen assets; this should be narrowed and conditioned on the full implementation of Iran’s commitments under the memorandum of understanding. As long as Iran remains in breach of those commitments, the Trump administration must insist that banks, businesses, and individuals across the region halt transfers to Iran.

To make sure such pressure has maximum effect, Israel and the United States need to increase cooperation. The two countries should immediately revive high-level interagency working groups so that they can hash out the details of the next phase of the cease-fire before leaders squabble publicly. These groups should focus on maintaining a credible military threat against Iran to prevent provocations, building a joint intelligence mechanism to detect and prevent the reconstruction of Iran’s nuclear and missile programs, coordinating sanctions enforcement, and developing a shared plan to defend Gulf infrastructure and maritime routes. Israel and the United States will continue to disagree on certain matters. By identifying those areas in advance, they can preserve pressure on Iran, avoid public rifts, and deny Tehran the opportunity to exploit gaps between them.

THE SAME PAGE

American and Israeli leaders can advance their joint goals—to prevent a nuclear Iran and the resurgence of Iran’s regional axis—by signing a framework that defines what they will do both during the 60-day window and afterward. Such a framework should include an intelligence campaign designed to detect any progress on Iran’s nuclear program, combining Israeli excellence in executing operations and cultivating human sources with U.S. satellite and financial intelligence. Israel and the United States could agree to a structured process for assessing Iranian decision-making, sanctions evasion, and nuclear and missile activity. This would also help ensure that intelligence is evaluated professionally and consistently, especially during periods of political pressure or military escalation. When evidence of a violation emerges, the two countries should be able to quickly assess it and coordinate a policy response.

Any U.S.-Israeli parallel agreement should also include an “if-then” matrix in which the two sides agree ahead of time on how they would handle Iranian violations. If Iran, for example, blocks nuclear inspectors, moves enriched material to military custody, or rebuilds hardened nuclear facilities, the United States should reimpose sanctions. If an Iranian proxy attacks Israel, U.S. forces, Gulf energy infrastructure, or international shipping, Israel and the United States should target not only the proxy but also the Iranian command, logistics, and financing architecture behind the attack.

A U.S.-Israeli agreement would not be a favor to Israel.

Under a U.S.-Israeli agreement, the two countries should also establish the parameters of an Iranian nuclear deal they could live with. Israel should not reject diplomacy with Iran as a matter of principle; it should oppose only an agreement that leaves Tehran with the capabilities, resources, or time needed to rebuild its nuclear program. The United States should guarantee it would sign only an Iranian nuclear deal that includes the removal of all enriched material from Iran, zero enrichment capacity, intensive inspections, no sunset clauses, and a mechanism for rapid access to suspicious sites. These standards are admittedly demanding, and Tehran has long resisted them, but they are imperative.

A U.S.-Israeli deal would not be complete without a long-term U.S. commitment to providing Israel with military aid. Israel desperately needs to replenish munitions, air and missile defense capacity, long-range strike capabilities, and pre-positioned U.S. stocks. Over the past few months, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has said that he plans to make sure his country no longer depends on American assistance. But his approach should now be reconsidered. A restored Israeli military would enable the United States to reduce its own footprint in the region without weakening deterrence against Iran or U.S. diplomatic efforts.

But that does not mean the kind of aid Washington provides should stay the same. In fact, in the long term, American military assistance should shift toward the joint research, development, and production of advanced weapons, including next-generation missile defense interceptors, potentially under Trump’s Golden Dome initiative. Even more important is the need for joint investment in a technological alliance that preserves American superiority in artificial intelligence, quantum computing, energy, semiconductors, and critical materials. This alliance should be established under a U.S.-Israeli memorandum and create a trusted ecosystem between the two countries, deepening cooperation among their private sectors, universities, and research institutions. This would produce a new model of partnership: one based not on patronage but on the shared power of each country and the strength of the alliance between them.

BACK TO SQUARE ONE?

It is in the U.S. interest to align with Israel through an agreement. The war demonstrated the value the United States derives from Israeli knowledge, infrastructure, and operational capabilities. A strong Israel reduces the chance that the United States itself will have to use force to pursue its regional goals. A U.S.-Israeli agreement would therefore not be a favor to Israel. It would be a way to preserve the gains of the war, share the burden of enforcing a deal with Tehran, and improve the credibility of U.S. commitments in the region.

If left unchecked, Iran will use the 60-day window established by its memorandum to exploit the daylight between Israel and the United States and rebuild the capabilities it needs to threaten the region—undermining the most important achievement of the Iran war. Israel will always prefer to work with Washington, but given the scale of the Iranian threat, Israel cannot depend on an ally to defend its own citizens.

A bad U.S.-Iranian deal would not automatically mean war. Israel, however, would be forced to act by itself should Iran pursue a nuclear weapon or should Hezbollah rebuild its military infrastructure or return to the border with Israel. With or without the United States, Israel’s intelligence apparatus will need to keep penetrating Iran’s highest ranks. It will need to remain capable of striking the country from far away, expand its missile defenses and civil resilience, quietly deepen coordination with Gulf states, and expose Iranian sanctions evasion. Israel has the right to use military force in self-defense if its redlines are about to be crossed.

For Israel, acting alone would be harder and riskier. But it is better than doing nothing. The country simply cannot allow diplomacy to become the cover under which Iran rebuilds capabilities that threaten Israel’s survival.
